The mismatch & fix — `volume.BlobPos`\n\n`volume.BlobPos` (`internal/volume/shape.go:1080`) was the sole outlier: bf-5v3q added the\ncanonical trio with **snake_case** JSON tags, while the sibling explainability.BlobSnapshot\n(bf-2ibc) explicitly used camelCase to match the dashboard. The Go struct field names were\nalready canonical (`PersonName`/`AssignedColor`/`IdentityResolved`); only the JSON tags\ndiverged.\n\n**Fix:** tags changed `person_name`→`personName`, `assigned_color`→`assignedColor`,\n`identity_resolved`→`identityResolved`; comment updated to cross-reference the dashboard\nalignment convention (mirroring `explainability.BlobSnapshot`).\n\n**Safety of the change:**\n- `volume.BlobPos` is **not** JSON-marshalled to the dashboard. It is consumed in Go:\n  `volume.Store.Evaluate(blobs []BlobPos) []string` returns fired-trigger IDs\n  (`shape.go:550`); `api.VolumeTriggersHandler.EvaluateTriggers` likewise returns `[]string`\n  (`volume_triggers.go:782`). Webhook/MQTT/notification payloads are built from separate\n  `volume.FiredEvent`-derived maps, not from `BlobPos` (`volume_triggers.go:579,885,967`).\n- The person-filter read is a Go field access (`blob.PersonName`), not a JSON decode — so the\n  tag change cannot affect trigger matching.\n- No test references the snake_case keys (verified: the only hits for `person_name`/\n  `assigned_color`/`identity_resolved` in `internal/volume/` were the struct-definition lines\n  themselves).\n- `go vet ./...` and `go test ./...` both pass after the change.\n\nThe tags were therefore inert today but would have leaked snake_case keys inconsistent with the\nTS interface the moment `BlobPos` is ever surfaced (debug endpoint, volume-trigger state API).\nAligning them removes that latent drift and makes the cross-check uniform.\n\n---\n\n## 4. TS declaration — confirmed (`dashboard/types/spaxel.d.ts`)\n\nThe `Blob` interface (`:10–91`) declares the canonical trio **and** the deprecated aliases:\n\n| Field | Line | JSON key | Status |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| `personName?: string` | 53 | `personName` | ✅ canonical |\n| `assignedColor?: string` | 74 | `assignedColor` | ✅ canonical |\n| `identityResolved?: boolean` | 90 | `identityResolved` | ✅ canonical (tri-state via `undefined`) |\n| `personLabel?: string` | 60 | `personLabel` | deprecated alias |\n| `personColor?: string` | 81 | `personColor` | deprecated alias |\n| `personId?: string` | 66 | `personId` | auxiliary |\n\n`spaxel.d.ts` is the **single declaration point** for the JS blob schema (bf-1q3m §6 Tier-4 #7);\nno JS file redeclares it.\n\n---\n\n## 2. TS ↔ Go JSON-tag mapping (the cross-check)\n\nThe canonical trio is carried by **8 Go types**. **7 emit camelCase** matching the TS; **1\n(`volume.BlobPos`) emitted snake_case** — the mismatch — and is fixed in this bead.\n\n| # | Go type | file:line | `personName` | `assignedColor` | `identityResolved` | Feeds dashboard? |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| 1 | `dashboard.blobJSON` | `internal/dashboard/hub.go:558-560` | `personName` ✅ | `assignedColor` ✅ | `identityResolved` (*bool) ✅ | **Yes — `/ws/dashboard` wire format** |\n| 2 | `api.Track` | `internal/api/tracks.go:31-33` | `personName` ✅ | `assignedColor` ✅ | `identityResolved` (*bool) ✅ | **Yes — `GET /api/tracks`** |\n| 3 | `explainability.BlobSnapshot` | `internal/explainability/handler.go:109-111` | `personName` ✅ | `assignedColor` ✅ | `identityResolved` (*bool) ✅ | **Yes — explainability REST** (via `BlobExplanation`, surfaced by `api/feedback.go:61`) |\n| 4 | `volume.BlobPos` | `internal/volume/shape.go:1090-1092` | ~~`person_name`~~ → `personName` 🔧 | ~~`assigned_color`~~ → `assignedColor` 🔧 | ~~`identity_resolved`~~ → `identityResolved` 🔧 | No (internal trigger-eval; `Evaluate` returns `[]string`) — see §3 |\n| 5 | `signal.TrackedBlob` | `internal/signal/processor.go:605-607` | `personName` ✅ | `assignedColor` ✅ | `identityResolved` (*bool) ✅ | Source struct (3D tracker feed) |\n| 6 | `tracker.Blob` (3D) | `internal/tracker/tracker.go:58-60` | `personName` ✅ | `assignedColor` ✅ | `identityResolved` (*bool) ✅ | Source (3D) |\n| 7 | `tracking.Blob` (2D) | `internal/tracking/tracker.go:45-47` | `personName` ✅ | `assignedColor` ✅ | `identityResolved` (*bool) ✅ | Source (2D, drives `blobJSON` via `BroadcastLocUpdate`) |\n| 8 | `automation.TrackedBlob` | `internal/automation/engine.go:1347-1349` | `personName` ✅ | `assignedColor` ✅ | `identityResolved` (*bool) ✅ | Engine-internal (Tier-1 #1 type; identity populated by a follow-up bead) |\n\n> **Deprecated aliases** (`person_label`/`person_color`, snake_case) are present on the source\n> types (#5–8) and on `blobJSON`/`api.Track` exactly as the TS declares them — consistent, no\n> change. The many other snake_case `person_id`/`person_name`/`person_label` fields elsewhere\n> (`events`, `notifications`, `ble.registry`, `analytics`, etc.) belong to unrelated\n> event/notification/registry types, **not** the canonical trio, and are out of scope.\n\n**Net result: all 8 types now emit the canonical trio as `personName`/`assignedColor`/\n`identityResolved`, exactly matching `spaxel.d.ts`.**\n\n---\n\n## 3. The mismatch & fix — `volume.BlobPos`\n\n`volume.BlobPos` (`internal/volume/shape.go:1080`) was the sole outlier: bf-5v3q added the\ncanonical trio with **snake_case** JSON tags, while the sibling explainability.BlobSnapshot\n(bf-2ibc) explicitly used camelCase to match the dashboard. The Go struct field names were\nalready canonical (`PersonName`/`AssignedColor`/`IdentityResolved`); only the JSON tags\ndiverged.\n\n**Fix:** tags changed `person_name`→`personName`, `assigned_color`→`assignedColor`,\n`identity_resolved`→`identityResolved`; comment updated to cross-reference the dashboard\nalignment convention (mirroring `explainability.BlobSnapshot`).\n\n**Safety of the change:**\n- `volume.BlobPos` is **not** JSON-marshalled to the dashboard. It is consumed in Go:\n  `volume.Store.Evaluate(blobs []BlobPos) []string` returns fired-trigger IDs\n  (`shape.go:550`); `api.VolumeTriggersHandler.EvaluateTriggers` likewise returns `[]string`\n  (`volume_triggers.go:782`). Webhook/MQTT/notification payloads are built from separate\n  `volume.FiredEvent`-derived maps, not from `BlobPos` (`volume_triggers.go:579,885,967`).\n- The person-filter read is a Go field access (`blob.PersonName`), not a JSON decode — so the\n  tag change cannot affect trigger matching.\n- No test references the snake_case keys (verified: the only hits for `person_name`/\n  `assigned_color`/`identity_resolved` in `internal/volume/` were the struct-definition lines\n  themselves).\n- `go vet ./...` and `go test ./...` both pass after the change.\n\nThe tags were therefore inert today but would have leaked snake_case keys inconsistent with the\nTS interface the moment `BlobPos` is ever surfaced (debug endpoint, volume-trigger state API).\nAligning them removes that latent drift and makes the cross-check uniform.\n\n---\n\n## 4. JS plumbing — confirmed\n\n### J1 — `dashboard/js/state.js:288-294` (creation path)\n```js\nfunction updateBlob(id, updates) {\n    if (!appState.blobs[id]) {\n        appState.blobs[id] = { id: id };          // ← :290  creation literal (id only)\n    }\n    Object.assign(appState.blobs[id], updates);   // ← :292  server payload merged in\n    ...\n}\n```\n✅ Confirmed. The creation literal is `{ id: id }` only; the server-supplied identity\n(`personName`/`assignedColor`/`identityResolved` from `blobJSON`) arrives in `updates` and is\nmerged by `Object.assign` at `:292`. **No pre-init needed** — no change.\n\n### J2 — `dashboard/js/websocket.js:167` (dead-reckoning cache)\n```js\n_blobStates.set(b.id, { x: b.x, z: b.z, vx: b.vx||0, vz: b.vz||0, ts: Date.now() });  // ← :167\n```\n✅ Confirmed identity-free by design. This is the `<5s` disconnect extrapolation cache\n(position + velocity only). It never carries identity. **Left alone.**\n\n---\n\n## 5. Acceptance criteria\n\n- [x] **spaxel.d.ts Blob interface confirmed** to declare `personName`, `assignedColor`,\n  `identityResolved` (+ deprecated `personLabel`/`personColor` aliases) — §1.\n- [x] **TS field names ↔ Go JSON-tag mapping documented; mismatch fixed** — §2 mapping table;\n  the single mismatch (`volume.BlobPos` snake_case) fixed in §3.\n- [x] **state.js:290 creation path confirmed** to surface server identity via `Object.assign`;\n  no change needed — §4 (J1).\n- [x] (Out-of-band confirmation) **websocket.js:167 (J2)** intentionally identity-free — §4 (J2).\n\n---\n\n## 6. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_ee6bca7605754a5ca8554e11","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t---\n2\tname: br-close-claimed-at-rfc3339\n3\tdescription: \"br close fails with \\\"Invalid claimed_at format: premature end of input\\\" — fix by converting worker_sessions timestamps to RFC3339\"\n4\tmetadata: \n5\t  node_type: memory\n6\t  type: reference\n7\t  originSessionId: 7f606246-9db4-4a7f-be95-650beddf4385\n8\t---\n9\t\n10\tIn the spaxel workspace, `br close <id>` fails with `Error: Invalid claimed_at format: premature end of input`. Root cause: bead-forge writes `worker_sessions.claimed_at` as a naive SQLite timestamp `'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS'` (space-separated, no timezone), but `br close` parses it strictly as RFC3339 and chokes. ALL `worker_sessions` rows had this bad format and ZERO beads had ever been closed here — so `br close` was globally broken. (It writes `closed_at` correctly as RFC3339; only the claim-write path is buggy.) bead `bf-49fp` had been auto-retried ~11,885 times because no agent could close it. Related to the bead-forge bug tracked in CLAUDE.md ([[bead-forge]] repair-fix bead bf-2yj7 family).\n11\t\n12\t**Why:** `br close` reads the most recent unclosed claim for the bead and parses `claimed_at`; one bad value aborts the whole close. Retrying the bead does not help — the failure is deterministic in the db.\n13\t\n14\t**How to apply:** When `br close` throws \"premature end of input\" / \"Invalid claimed_at format\":\n15\t1. `br sync --flush-only` — checkpoint bead content (issues/comments) to JSONL.\n16\t2. Back up the db: `cp .beads/beads.db .beads/beads.db.bak`.\n17\t3. Convert only the naive rows (leaving valid RFC3339 untouched):\n18\t   ```python\n19\t   import sqlite3, re\n20\t   con = sqlite3.connect('.beads/beads.db')\n21\t   naive = re.compile(r'^\\d{4}-\\d{2}-\\d{2} \\d{2}:\\d{2}:\\d{2}$')\n22\t   rows = con.execute('SELECT rowid, claimed_at FROM worker_sessions').fetchall()\n23\t   con.execute('BEGIN')\n24\t   for rid, ts in rows:\n25\t       if naive.match(ts or ''):\n26\t           con.execute('UPDATE worker_sessions SET claimed_at=? WHERE rowid=?',\n27\t                       (ts[:10]+'T'+ts[11:]+'+00:00', rid))\n28\t   con.commit()\n29\t   ```\n30\t   (UTC confirmed safe: most recent claim's wall-clock matches `issues.updated_at` which is `+00:00`.)\n31\t4. `br close <id>` now succeeds; `br sync --flush-only`; remove the `.bak`.\n32\tNOTE: only the `worker_sessions` table (dispatch telemetry) is touched — never the `issues` table (bead content) or comments.\n33\t\n34\t**Update (2026-07-06):** the *global* break is fixed — 12269 of ~12271 `worker_sessions` rows are now valid RFC3339 and beads do close. But the claim-write bug **persists for new dispatches**: a fresh claim still writes `claimed_at` as naive `CURRENT_TIMESTAMP` (`YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS`, length 19), so expect ~1–2 bad rows per new dispatch — the targeted repair above is **recurring**, not one-time. Confirmed again on bf-18yn (two length-19 rows from today's dispatch blocked the close; converting them unblocked it). The `sqlite3` binary IS available via Nix at `/nix/store/*-sqlite-3.51.2-bin/bin/sqlite3` (not on PATH) — a one-liner repair works:\n35\t```bash\n36\tSQLITE=$(ls -d /nix/store/*-sqlite-*-bin/bin/sqlite3 | head -1)\n37\t$SQLITE .beads/beads.db \"UPDATE worker_sessions SET claimed_at=strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S+00:00',claimed_at) WHERE claimed_at LIKE '____-__-__ __:__:__';\"\n38\t```\n39\t(`python3 -c \"import sqlite3...\"` still works as a fallback if no Nix sqlite3 is present.)\n40\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"8a21348d-2c61-4267-810e-8a15d429d992","uuid":"612ffd70-3c54-4c21-9f93-2a9c51ce9ded","timestamp":"2026-07-06T21:59:15.959Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/coding/.claude/projects/-home-coding-spaxel/memory/br-close-claimed-at-rfc3339.md","content":"---\nname: br-close-claimed-at-rfc3339\ndescription: \"br close fails with \\\"Invalid claimed_at format: premature end of input\\\" — fix by converting worker_sessions timestamps to RFC3339\"\nmetadata: \n  node_type: memory\n  type: reference\n  originSessionId: 7f606246-9db4-4a7f-be95-650beddf4385\n---\n\nIn the spaxel workspace, `br close <id>` fails with `Error: Invalid claimed_at format: premature end of input`. Root cause: bead-forge writes `worker_sessions.claimed_at` as a naive SQLite timestamp `'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS'` (space-separated, no timezone), but `br close` parses it strictly as RFC3339 and chokes. ALL `worker_sessions` rows had this bad format and ZERO beads had ever been closed here — so `br close` was globally broken. (It writes `closed_at` correctly as RFC3339; only the claim-write path is buggy.) bead `bf-49fp` had been auto-retried ~11,885 times because no agent could close it. Related to the bead-forge bug tracked in CLAUDE.md ([[bead-forge]] repair-fix bead bf-2yj7 family).\n\n**Why:** `br close` reads the most recent unclosed claim for the bead and parses `claimed_at`; one bad value aborts the whole close. Retrying the bead does not help — the failure is deterministic in the db.\n\n**How to apply:** When `br close` throws \"premature end of input\" / \"Invalid claimed_at format\":\n1. `br sync --flush-only` — checkpoint bead content (issues/comments) to JSONL.\n2. Back up the db: `cp .beads/beads.db .beads/beads.db.bak`.\n3. Convert only the naive rows (leaving valid RFC3339 untouched):\n   ```python\n   import sqlite3, re\n   con = sqlite3.connect('.beads/beads.db')\n   naive = re.compile(r'^\\d{4}-\\d{2}-\\d{2} \\d{2}:\\d{2}:\\d{2}$')\n   rows = con.execute('SELECT rowid, claimed_at FROM worker_sessions').fetchall()\n   con.execute('BEGIN')\n   for rid, ts in rows:\n       if naive.match(ts or ''):\n           con.execute('UPDATE worker_sessions SET claimed_at=? WHERE rowid=?',\n                       (ts[:10]+'T'+ts[11:]+'+00:00', rid))\n   con.commit()\n   ```\n   (UTC confirmed safe: most recent claim's wall-clock matches `issues.updated_at` which is `+00:00`.)\n4. `br close <id>` now succeeds; `br sync --flush-only`; remove the `.bak`.\nNOTE: only the `worker_sessions` table (dispatch telemetry) is touched — never the `issues` table (bead content) or comments.\n\n**Update (2026-07-06):** the *global* break is fixed — 12269 of ~12271 `worker_sessions` rows are now valid RFC3339 and beads do close. But the claim-write bug **persists for new dispatches**: a fresh claim still writes `claimed_at` as naive `CURRENT_TIMESTAMP` (`YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS`, length 19), so expect ~1–2 bad rows per new dispatch — the targeted repair above is **recurring**, not one-time. Confirmed again on bf-18yn (two length-19 rows from today's dispatch blocked the close; converting them unblocked it). The `sqlite3` binary IS available via Nix at `/nix/store/*-sqlite-3.51.2-bin/bin/sqlite3` (not on PATH) — a one-liner repair works:\n```bash\nSQLITE=$(ls -d /nix/store/*-sqlite-*-bin/bin/sqlite3 | head -1)\n$SQLITE .beads/beads.db \"UPDATE worker_sessions SET claimed_at=strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S+00:00',claimed_at) WHERE claimed_at LIKE '____-__-__ __:__:__';\"\n```\n(`python3 -c \"import sqlite3...\"` still works as a fallback if no Nix sqlite3 is present.)\n","numLines":40,"startLine":1,"totalLines":40}}}
